reason: Wren/Dunnock/Robin all spend significant time on the ground beneath shrubs and in leaf litter in addition to canopy perching.
White truffle is a subterranean mycorrhizal fungus fruiting underground near roots — not on bark surfaces.
Oak aphids are sessile sap-feeders on leaves and shoots; they do not visit flowers and barely move positionally.
Tuberolachnus forms stationary colonies on willow bark stems; Chaitophorus feeds on leaves but neither visits flowers.
Song Thrush/Redwing/Fieldfare all alternate between fruiting canopy and ground earthworm foraging in Amsterdam parks.
Redwing and Fieldfare strip hawthorn berries from canopies then forage for earthworms on open park lawns.
Turdus guild around Nyssa trees splits time between fruiting canopy and ground invertebrate foraging.
Blackbird is primarily a ground forager; ground-park dominates with tree-canopy secondary for roosting and berry feeding.
Blackbird and Song Thrush both forage heavily on the ground beneath Malus trees for fallen fruit and invertebrates.
Song and Mistle Thrushes feed on Amelanchier berries in canopy then use stone anvils on the ground to crack snails.
Fieldfare and Redwing winter flocks strip Sorbus berries from canopy then work park lawns for earthworms.
Fieldfare and Redwing on Malus behave identically to other Sorbus/hawthorn contexts — canopy feeding plus ground foraging.
Mistle Thrush defends berry trees aggressively and also strides boldly across open lawns for large earthworms.
These moths (hawk-moth/tussock/pug/Chinese character) are larval tree feeders and day-resting adults — no flower visiting.
Mice/voles/foxes/badgers are all ground-park mammals well-covered by the category default.
Geometrid moth caterpillars feed on Sorbus foliage; adults rest motionless on bark — neither stage visits flowers.
Verticillium is a soil-borne root pathogen; it has no above-ground fruiting body so ground-park overrides tree-bark default.
Wasps and hoverflies are both flower visitors but hoverflies hover distinctively and wasps use sustained transit flight.
Vespula hunt caterpillars in canopy and nest in ground; parasitoid wasps locate hosts in foliage with active hunting flights.
Fox/badger/boar are all ground-park frugivores well-covered by the mammal category default.
Red Fox and Hedgehog are classic ground-park mammals that forage through Amsterdam parks and gardens.
Caterpillars feed on Pterocarya buds/leaves in canopy; female winter moths crawl up trunks rather than visiting flowers.
Wood Pigeon/Magpie/Carrion Crow are genuine urban generalists using pavements/rooftops/lawns equally with tree canopy.
Carpenter bees forage on Cercis/Platanus flowers and nest in dry timber structures; powerful transit flight between patches.
Violet carpenter bee visits Catalpa flowers and nests in dry wood; strong flier making sky-looper appropriate for transit.
Ambrosia beetle lives entirely inside bark/wood galleries; flower-bobber default does not apply to this wood-boring beetle.
Longhorn beetle larvae bore into Morus wood; adults rest on bark or chew twigs — no flower visiting at any stage.
Ermine moth caterpillars web-encase Prunus canopy; adults rest on bark — conspicuous tree organisms with no flower-visiting stage.
Wood leopard moth larvae bore into Populus wood for years; adults rest on bark at day — classic tree-bark overriding insect default.
